trends.name: Embedding outliers + steering
trends.summary: This trend groups papers around steering, diffusion, activation steering, with representative work on Train Once, Reuse Everywhere: Generalizable Implicit ICL by Routing Attention.
representativeSentences: Large language models can be steered at inference time through prompting or activation interventions, but activation steering methods often underperform compared to prompt-based approaches.

To this end, we introduce Prompt Steering Replacement (PSR) models, a new family of activation steering methods that distill prompt steering behavior into interpretable interventions on model activations.
We investigate whether activation steering can be improved by learning to mimic the interventions that prompt steering triggers within the model.

trends.name: Cluster 01: causal / learning + conformal
trends.summary: This trend groups papers around conformal, conditional, conformal prediction, with representative work on On Testing Conditional Mean Independence for Manifold-Valued Data.
representativeSentences: Distinct from approaches that target relaxed notions of conditional coverage, we directly minimize the mean squared error of conditional coverage by refining the quantile regression components that underpin many conformal methods.
While conformal prediction provides robust marginal coverage guarantees, achieving reliable conditional coverage for specific inputs remains challenging.
We propose Localized Spatial Conformal Prediction (LSCP), a model-agnostic framework that bridges this gap by coupling local quantile regression with conformal calibration.

trends.name: Cluster 02: decoding / diffusion + decoding
trends.summary: This trend groups papers around decoding, unmasking, diffusion, with representative work on Draft-Conditioned Constrained Decoding for Structured Generation in LLMs.
representativeSentences: Masked diffusion language models decode by iteratively unmasking tokens, where the unmasking order defines an ``order of thought'' that strongly influences generation quality yet is typically chosen heuristically.
We propose training-free parallel decoding for rollout generation in diffusion large language model (dLLM) policy optimization, reducing rollout cost without auxiliary models or policy modification.
Masked Diffusion Models (MDMs) offer greater flexibility in decoding order than autoregressive models but require careful path planning to achieve high-quality generation.

trends.name: Cluster 04: attacks / adversarial + backdoor
trends.summary: This trend groups papers around backdoor, attacks, attack, with representative work on From Parameters to Feature Space: Task Arithmetic for Backdoor Mitigation in Model Merging.
representativeSentences: Extensive experiments demonstrate that LFPM consistently exhibits strong robustness against backdoor attacks in both full fine-tuning and Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) settings.
Empirically, BYORn consistently improves robustness to backdoor attacks while preserving clean-task performance, establishing a new trade-off frontier between generalization and attack success rate.
Recent work has shown that this paradigm is highly vulnerable to backdoor attacks, and that existing defenses are ineffective in open-ended generation settings.

trends.name: Cluster 03: regret / algorithm + regret
trends.summary: This trend groups papers around regret, mathcal, bound, with representative work on Contextual Slate GLM Bandits with Limited Adaptivity.
representativeSentences: Our algorithm is both efficient and achieves an optimal regret bound of $\widetilde{O}(\sqrt{ K|\mathcal{A}|\log|\mathcal{F}|})$, where $K$ is the number of rounds, $\mathcal{A}$ is the set of arms, and $\mathcal{F}$ is the function class used to approximate the losses.
A crucial observation is that the first-order dependence on movement costs in our regret bound plays a key role in enabling optimal comparator-adaptive dynamic regret guarantees in both settings.

In particular, we introduce a simple and provably efficient algorithm, \emph{Recursive Trajectory-based Preference Q-Learning} (RTPQ), and establish its regret bound while explicitly accounting for the trajectory-level and comparative structure of preferences.

trends.name: Cluster 06: graph / learning + graph
trends.summary: This trend groups papers around graph, node, graphs, with representative work on L2G-NET: Local to Global Spectral Graph Neural Networks via Cauchy Factorizations.
representativeSentences: Learning on graphs with missing node attributes is a prevalent yet challenging problem in real-world scenarios, as graph neural networks (GNNs) typically rely on complete attribute information.
Recent efforts to integrate Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) and Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown promise for learning on TAGs, yet achieving well-aligned representations remains challenging.
Representation learning on text-attributed graphs (TAGs) is crucial for real-world applications, as it enables effective modeling of both rich node semantics and complex graph structure.

trends.name: Cluster 05: video / geometric + motion
trends.summary: This trend groups papers around motion, gaussian splatting, video, with representative work on E²I-VRWKV: Explicit EPI-Representation and Interaction-Aware Vision-RWKV for Light Field Sema....
representativeSentences: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) enables real-time, high-fidelity view synthesis via explicit scene representations and has recently been extended to dynamic scene modeling.
However, lifting dense 2D foundation-model embeddings into 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) is still challenging.
Recent neural methods, such as 3D Gaussian Splatting, have achieved state-of-the-art rendering quality and speed.

trends.name: Cluster 07: neural / learning + neural
trends.summary: This trend groups papers around neural, pdes, pinns, with representative work on Origo: Physically Interpretable Multi-Physics PDE Pre-training through Neural Operator Splitting.
representativeSentences: We study the posterior contraction rate of Bayesian Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) for solving a general class of elliptic partial differential equations (PDEs).
Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) solve time-dependent partial differential equations (PDEs) by learning a mesh-free, differentiable solution that can be evaluated anywhere in space and time.

Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) can be unreliable on PDEs with oscillatory, multiscale, stiff, or long-time solutions, and these difficulties worsen in high dimensions where collocation-based training yields large numerical integration error and high-variance gradients.

trends.name: Cluster 08: flow / generative + flow
trends.summary: This trend groups papers around flow, flow matching, matching, with representative work on Rethinking the Design Space of Reinforcement Learning for Diffusion Models: On the Importance....
representativeSentences: To address this, we propose Inverse Poisson Flow Matching (IPFM), a novel distillation framework that accelerates electrostatic generative models across all values of $D$.

Recent flow matching methods on general manifolds often relies on approximating geodesics without closed forms, suffers from limited theoretical and implementation grounding, or trades generation quality to learn velocity fields.
We propose a training-free conditional sampling method for flow matching models based on importance sampling.

trends.name: Cluster 13: attention / memory + attention
trends.summary: This trend groups papers around attention, memory, sparse attention, with representative work on Learning When to Attend: Conditional Memory Access for Long-Context LLMs.
representativeSentences: Based on this insight, we propose LoSA (Locality-aware Sparse Attention), which reuses cached prefix-attention results for stable tokens and applies sparse attention only to active tokens with large representation changes.
Moreover, L2A enables post-training pruning of highly sparse Global Attention layers, reducing KV cache memory by up to 50\% with negligible performance loss.

We evaluate L2A on Qwen 2.5 and Qwen 3 models, extending their effective context length from 32K to 128K tokens, where it matches standard long-context training within 1.5–3\% while skipping Global Attention for $\sim$80\% of tokens and outperforming prior baselines.

trends.name: Cluster 10: safety / agents + safety
trends.summary: This trend groups papers around safety, reasoning, attack, with representative work on It's a TRAP! Task-Redirecting Agent Persuasion Benchmark for Web Agents.
representativeSentences: Their reliance on dynamic web content, however, makes them vulnerable to prompt injection attacks: adversarial instructions hidden in interface elements that persuade the agent to divert from its original task.

Existing alignment methods, largely optimized for static generation and task completion, break down in these settings due to sequential decision-making, adversarial tool feedback, and overconfident intermediate reasoning.
We introduce MOSAIC, a post-training framework that aligns agents for safe multi-step tool use by making safety decisions explicit and learnable.

trends.name: Cluster 11: time / forecasting + forecasting
trends.summary: This trend groups papers around forecasting, time series, series, with representative work on L-Drive: Beyond a Single Mapping—Latent Context Drives Time Series Forecasting.
representativeSentences: However, existing methods for time series forecasting with exogenous variables (TSF-X) have the following shortcomings: 1) they do not leverage future exogenous variables, 2) they fail to fully account for the correlation between endogenous and exogenous variables.

Time series forecasting plays a crucial role in a wide range of real-world applications and has become increasingly complex with the growth of multivariate dimensions and extended historical observations, leading to the prosperity of deep forecasting models.
Time Series Foundation Models (TSFMs) have achieved strong performance in univariate time series forecasting.
